q: What does effective stress σ' represent in soil mechanics?
opts: Total stress minus pore water pressure
Total stress plus pore water pressure
explain: Effective stress = σ - u (Terzaghi 1925). It is the stress carried by the soil skeleton, controlling ALL mechanical behavior: shear strength, consolidation rate, and liquefaction resistance.

q: What is the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion for drained conditions?
explain: Drained: τf = c' + σ'tanφ'. Both c' (cohesion) and φ' (friction angle) are effective stress parameters from CD triaxial or drained direct shear tests. Undrained: τf = cu (φu=0).

q: In Terzaghi's consolidation theory, what does Tv = 0.848 correspond to?
explain: Time factors: Tv=0.197 → U=50%. Tv=0.848 → U=90%. Tv=1.781 → U=99%. The formula: Tv = cv·t/H² where H is the drainage path length. Double drainage: H = half layer thickness.

q: Liquefaction is most likely to occur in which soil type?
explain: Liquefaction requires: (1) cohesionless soil (sand/silt), (2) saturated (below water table), (3) loose state (low Dr or low N), (4) cyclic loading (earthquake). FL = CRR/CSR < 1.0 → liquefied.

q: What is the typical friction angle φ' for loose to medium dense clean sand?
explain: Sand: φ' = 28-32° (loose, rounded), 32-38° (medium dense), 38-45° (dense, angular). Clay: φ' = 20-30°. φ' depends on particle shape, size distribution, and state of compaction. From triaxial CD tests.

q: What happens to effective stress during undrained loading of saturated clay?
opts: Excess pore pressure develops, effective stress doesn't change much short-term
explain: In undrained loading, volume change is prevented. Excess pore pressure develops to carry the applied load. Total stress increases but σ' changes little initially. Over time (consolidation), u dissipates and σ' increases.

q: The compression index Cc is used for calculating:
explain: Sc = Cc·H·log(σ'f/σ'0)/(1+e0). Cc from oedometer test (slope of e vs log σ' curve in virgin compression). Cs = swelling/recompression index (used for OCR > 1). Typically Cc ≈ 0.009(LL-10) for remolded clays.

q: What does OCR > 1 indicate?
explain: OCR = σ'pc/σ'v0. OCR = 1: normally consolidated (NC). OCR > 1: overconsolidated (OC) — soil was previously under higher stress (e.g., erosion, past ice load). OC soils are denser and stronger than NC at same σ'v0.

q: What is Darcy's Law for flow through soil?
explain: v = k·i where v = seepage velocity (m/s), k = hydraulic conductivity (m/s), i = hydraulic gradient (dimensionless). Q = k·i·A. Clay: k ≈ 10⁻⁸-10⁻¹⁰ m/s. Sand: k ≈ 10⁻³-10⁻⁵ m/s.

q: What is the general bearing capacity equation (Terzaghi)?
explain: Terzaghi (1943): qu = cNc + qNq + 0.5γBNγ. Nc, Nq, Nγ depend on φ'. For φ'=30°: Nc=30.1, Nq=18.4, Nγ=15.7. Allowable: qa = qu/FS. FS = 3 for static loads. Shape factors applied for non-strip footings.

q: What is the formula for P-wave velocity?
explain: Vp = √(M/ρ) where M = constrained modulus = K + 4G/3 = λ + 2G. Always Vp > Vs. In saturated soil, K ≫ G so Vp is dominated by bulk stiffness (~1500 m/s like water). Vs is unaffected by saturation (G same).

q: Why can't S-waves travel through water or liquefied soil?
explain: S-waves require shear stiffness G. Fluids (water, liquefied soil) have G = 0 — they cannot resist shear. Therefore Vs = √(G/ρ) = 0 in fluids. This is why S-waves don't travel through water and why Vs indicates liquefaction.

q: What is Vs30 and what is it used for?
opts: Average S-wave velocity in top 30m for site classification
explain: Vs30 = 30/Σ(hi/Vsi) = time-average Vs for top 30m. Used for seismic site classification worldwide. Japan: 告示1457号. NEHRP classes: A(>1500), B(760-1500), C(360-760), D(180-360), E(<180) m/s.

q: Snell's Law for seismic refraction is expressed as:
explain: sin(θ)/V = constant (ray parameter). At critical angle ic: sin(ic) = V₁/V₂. Head wave travels along interface at V₂. Travel time: t = x/V₁ + 2h·cos(ic)/V₁. Crossover distance: xc = 2h√((V₂-V₁)/(V₂+V₁)).

q: In a PS logging survey, which is measured?
opts: Both Vp and Vs vs depth in a borehole
explain: PS logging (PS検層, JGS 3511) measures BOTH Vp and Vs at depth intervals in a borehole. Surface source (sledgehammer), 3-component downhole receiver. Output: Vp(z), Vs(z) → Vs30, G0, E0, ν.

q: The small-strain shear modulus G0 from Vs is:
explain: G0 = ρ·Vs² where ρ = mass density (kg/m³), Vs = shear wave velocity (m/s). G0 in Pa. Example: ρ=1800 kg/m³, Vs=200 m/s → G0 = 1800×200² = 72 MPa. G0 = small-strain (dynamic) modulus, larger than static G.

q: Rayleigh wave velocity VR in homogeneous material is approximately:
explain: VR ≈ 0.92·Vs for ν=0.25. Retrograde elliptical particle motion. In layered media, VR is dispersive (different frequency = different depth = different Vs). MASW exploits this dispersion to build Vs profiles.

q: Seismic refraction CANNOT detect which condition?
opts: Velocity inversion (low-speed layer under high-speed layer)
explain: Seismic refraction requires velocity increasing with depth (V₂>V₁). A velocity inversion (low-speed layer beneath high-speed layer) creates a 'hidden layer' — the refraction method cannot detect it. Use reflection or tomography instead.

q: What is the typical Vs of soft clay?
explain: Soft clay: Vs = 50-150 m/s (very soft). Dense sand: 200-400. Soft rock: 400-700. Hard rock: >1000. Tokyo bay area soft alluvial clays often have Vs < 100 m/s → site class D → high amplification.

q: The H/V spectral ratio peak frequency in microtremor surveys corresponds to:
explain: H/V (Nakamura method 1989): H/V peak = f0 = site fundamental frequency (T0 = 1/f0). Related to average Vs and depth H: f0 ≈ Vs/4H. High H/V amplitude at f0 → strong site amplification expected.

q: In SPT, what does the N-value represent?
opts: Number of blows per 300mm to drive the sampler
explain: N = number of blows to drive split-spoon sampler 300 mm (after 150 mm seating drive). Hammer: 63.5 kg, drop 760 mm. JIS A 1219. N60 = N×(ER/60) corrects for energy ratio. Japanese auto hammer: ER ≈ 72%.

q: The overburden correction factor CN for SPT is applied to:
explain: CN corrects N60 to reference pressure (100 kPa). CN = √(Pa/σ'v0). (N1)60 = CN×N60. Deep samples appear lower N due to confinement. Correction brings all values to same confining stress for comparison.

q: In CPTu, what does Ic represent?
explain: Ic = √((3.47-logQt)²+(1.22+logFr)²) — Soil Behavior Type Index (Robertson 1990). Ic < 1.31: gravels. 1.31-2.05: clean sands. 2.05-2.60: silty sands. 2.60-2.95: sandy silts. 2.95-3.60: silty clays. >3.60: clays.

q: Wenner array apparent resistivity formula is:
explain: Wenner: ρa = 2πa·(V/I) where a = electrode spacing, V = voltage between MN, I = injected current. K = 2πa (geometric factor). Larger a → deeper penetration (approximately 0.5a). Good for horizontal mapping.

q: PS logging JGS 3511 measures:
opts: Dynamic P and S wave velocities in borehole
explain: PS検層 (JGS 3511): 3-component geophone moved down borehole in 1m steps. Surface hammer source on plank (P) or horizontal (S). First-arrival picks → interval Vp and Vs. Used for Vs30, G0=ρVs², seismic design.

q: The FL method for liquefaction assessment uses:
explain: FL = CRR/CSR (道路橋示方書, Japanese Road Bridge Specification). FL < 1.0: liquefiable. FL = 1.0-1.5: marginal. FL > 1.5: safe. CRR from (N1)60cs or Vs. CSR = 0.65×(σv/σ'v)×(amax/g)×rd.

q: What is the critical angle in seismic refraction?
explain: ic = arcsin(V₁/V₂). At this angle of incidence, the refracted ray travels along the interface. Below ic: reflection occurs. At ic: head wave forms. Refraction works only when V₂ > V₁ (otherwise no ic exists).

q: What does a borehole camera (孔内カメラ) primarily reveal that a core log alone cannot?
opts: 3D fracture orientation, aperture, and groundwater inflow locations
explain: Borehole cameras provide visual images of fracture orientation (dip/strike), aperture (crack width), infill material, weathering grade, and exact depth of groundwater inflow. Core samples lose orientation information during retrieval — the camera sees these features in place.

q: In a pressuremeter test, what does the limit pressure PL represent?
opts: The lateral ground failure pressure (ultimate resistance)
explain: PL = limit pressure is the asymptotic pressure at which the soil/rock fails laterally during pressuremeter inflation. It is analogous to bearing capacity in the horizontal direction. PL ≈ 5.5×cu for soft clay. Used for pile lateral capacity and foundation design.

q: What is the K30 value in a plate load test and what is the Japanese road base specification?
opts: K30 is depth of test; spec ≥ 150 MN/m³
K30 = settlement at 300 kPa; spec ≥ 10 mm
K30 = pressure/settlement at 1.25mm; Japanese road base spec ≥ 110 MN/m³
explain: K30 = subgrade reaction coefficient from plate load test. K30 = load at 1.25mm settlement / 1.25mm, using a 300mm diameter rigid plate. Japanese road design specification (道路橋示方書): K30 ≥ 110 MN/m³ for road base layer. Higher K30 = stiffer subgrade.

q: RQD = 55% on a core log is classified as:
explain: RQD classification: 90-100% = Excellent (優良), 75-90% = Good (良), 50-75% = Fair (普通), 25-50% = Poor (不良), <25% = Very Poor (極めて不良). RQD = 55% falls in FAIR (50-75%). Tunnel support: steel sets + shotcrete likely needed. RMR will reflect this classification.

q: Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) works BEST in which ground condition?
explain: GPR signal is attenuated by high electrical conductivity. Saltwater, wet clay, and conductive soils absorb the EM energy — depth penetration drops to 0.5-1m. Dry sand, gravel, and dry rock are ideal (low conductivity) — depth up to 10-30m possible. Always check soil conductivity before deploying GPR.

q: In GPR, the depth formula d = v·t/2 uses two-way travel time because:
opts: The radar travels at double speed underground
The pulse travels DOWN to the reflector and BACK UP — hence ÷2 for one-way depth
The antenna moves twice per scan
explain: The radar pulse travels from surface antenna DOWN to the reflector, then reflects UP back to the same antenna. The recorded time t is two-way travel time (TWTT). Depth = (wave velocity × TWTT) / 2. Velocity v = c/√εr where εr is the soil dielectric constant.

q: Which investigation method is the ONLY standard way to directly measure the lateral stiffness (horizontal deformation modulus) of soil or soft rock?
opts: Pressuremeter test (孔内水平載荷試験)
explain: The pressuremeter test (Ménard) is the ONLY standard in-situ test that directly measures horizontal stress-strain behavior. It inflates a cylindrical probe against the borehole wall and records pressure vs. volume change. Em (pressuremeter modulus) is used directly in Ménard's settlement formula for laterally loaded piles and flexible foundations.

q: What is the key difference between RQD and TCR (Total Core Recovery)?
opts: RQD counts pieces ≥100mm only (natural breaks); TCR counts ALL recovered core
TCR is for soft soil; RQD is for hard rock
RQD is measured in % while TCR is in mm
explain: RQD = (Σ intact pieces ≥ 100mm natural breaks) / L × 100%. TCR = all recovered core / L × 100%. TCR ≥ RQD always. A core with many short pieces (all <100mm) could have TCR=100% but RQD=0%. Only natural fractures are counted for RQD — mechanical drill breaks are excluded.
